sections.header: Verses 1–17 — "Cursed Is the One Who Does Not Obey"

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to proclaim "the terms of this covenant" (dibrê habbĕrît, v.2). The covenant in view is the Sinai covenant — specifically the curses of Deuteronomy 27–28. The "iron-smelting furnace" (kûr habbarzel, v.4) recalls the Exodus from Egypt, grounding the covenant demand in redemptive history. Bĕrît is not a negotiated contract but a sovereign imposition: God sets the terms, and Israel's response is either blessing or curse.

The word ʾārûr ("cursed," v.3) echoes the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 27:15–26, where twelve curses are pronounced for specific violations. Jeremiah invokes these curses as now operative — the covenant has been broken, and the curses are in effect.

panels.ctx: Chapter 11 explicitly invokes the Sinai/Deuteronomic covenant for the first time in Jeremiah. The "terms of this covenant" (v.2) likely refers to the Book of the Law discovered during Josiah's reforms (2 Kgs 22). The chapter reveals that despite Josiah's revival, the covenant was still being broken — not merely by individuals but by a "conspiracy" (qešer, v.9) among the people of Judah and Jerusalem. The olive tree metaphor (vv.16–17) anticipates Paul's olive tree allegory in Romans 11.

cross.note: "Cursed is anyone who does not uphold the words of this law." Jeremiah invokes the formal covenant curse structure.
Paul's olive tree allegory — branches broken off for unbelief — develops the same image Jeremiah introduces.
Paul cites the Deuteronomic curse: "Cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the Book of the Law." The same covenant theology.

notes.note: God commands Jeremiah to proclaim the covenant terms — not a new covenant but the Sinai covenant with its blessings and curses. The "iron-smelting furnace" recalls Egypt and grounds the demand in redemptive grace: "I saved you; now obey."
A "conspiracy" (qešer) — the same word used for political revolts — describes Judah's coordinated rejection of God. This is not individual backsliding but organized, systematic covenant violation.
"Do not pray for this people" — the prohibition from 7:16 is repeated. Intercession is again suspended; the decree is sealed.

Israel was once a "thriving olive tree" — beautiful, fruitful. Now God himself will set it on fire. The imagery is agricultural catastrophe: the most valuable tree in the orchards destroyed by its own owner.

Calvin emphasises that the covenant demand is grounded in prior grace. God does not demand obedience from strangers but from redeemed people. The curse falls on those who have received blessing and then spurned it.

Calvin again wrestles with the prayer prohibition: "God closes the door of intercession not because he delights in wrath but because the case is beyond remedy. Even prayer cannot change a decision that has been finally sealed."
The "iron-smelting furnace" (kûr habbarzel) is a metaphor for Egypt as a place of suffering that produced a refined people — like metal purified by extreme heat.
This verse is notoriously difficult in Hebrew. The MT seems to ask: "What is my beloved doing in my temple?" — personifying Israel as a faithless bride who desecrates the sanctuary with her schemes.

Lundbom identifies this as a "covenant enforcement oracle" — Jeremiah functions as a covenant prosecutor, reading the terms of the agreement and demonstrating systematic violation. The legal framework is Deuteronomic, but the application is contemporary.

Brueggemann reads the covenant oracle as a confrontation with Judah's "contractual theology" — the belief that God owes protection regardless of behavior. Jeremiah insists that covenant is bilateral: obligations bind both parties, and Israel has defaulted.

sections.header: Verses 18–23 — The Plot Against Jeremiah at Anathoth

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah describes himself as "a gentle lamb (kebeś) led to the slaughter" (v.19), unaware of the plots against his life. The image prefigures Isaiah 53:7 — the Suffering Servant "led like a lamb to the slaughter." Jeremiah's innocent suffering at the hands of his own townspeople becomes a type of Christ's passion.

panels.ctx: The Anathoth plot reveals that Jeremiah's own relatives and neighbors are conspiring to kill him. This is the first of Jeremiah's "confessions" — personal laments addressed to God about the cost of prophetic ministry. The men of Anathoth threaten: "Do not prophesy in the name of the LORD or you will die by our hands" (v.21). God's response is judgment on Anathoth itself — the assassins will be punished.

cross.note: "Like a lamb led to the slaughter" — Isaiah's Suffering Servant language echoes Jeremiah's self-description.
"A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown" — Jesus' experience mirrors Jeremiah's rejection at Anathoth.

notes.note: Jeremiah's innocence is emphasized: he was like a "gentle lamb" unaware of the plot. The conspiracy came from his own hometown — Anathoth, the priestly village where he grew up. The betrayal is deeply personal.

God pronounces judgment on the men of Anathoth: their young men will die by sword, their sons and daughters by famine. No remnant will survive. The punishment fits the crime: they tried to silence God's word and will themselves be silenced.

Calvin draws the typological connection: "Jeremiah is a figure of Christ, who was delivered as a lamb to his enemies by his own people." The prophet's suffering anticipates the greater suffering of the one he foreshadows.
The phrase "like a gentle lamb" (kĕkebeś ʾallûp) uses the adjective ʾallûp, which can mean "tame/docile" or "trusting" — emphasising Jeremiah's naive trust in those who were plotting his death.

Lundbom classifies this as the first of Jeremiah's "confessions" — a genre unique in prophetic literature. Unlike the psalmic laments they resemble, these confessions arise from the specific tension of prophetic vocation: the prophet must announce judgment on people he loves.

Brueggemann identifies a pattern that will recur throughout the confessions: (1) the prophet discovers a plot, (2) the prophet appeals to God, (3) God pronounces judgment on the plotters. The pattern reveals that prophetic authority is authenticated not by success but by suffering.

rows.label: vv.14–17
rows.text: No Intercession: The Olive Tree Burns

rows.label: vv.18–23
rows.text: First Confession: The Plot at Anathoth

lit.note: Chapter 11 pairs covenant theology (vv.1–17) with personal suffering (vv.18–23). The broken covenant produces not only national judgment but persecution of the prophet who announces it.

themes.note: Covenant peaks at 10 — this is the explicit covenant-enforcement chapter. Judgment at 9 follows from broken covenant; Prophecy at 8 reflects the prophet's persecution.

Bĕrît is not a negotiated contract but a sovereign imposition: God sets the terms, and Israel's response is either blessing or curse.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Broken Covenant

translations.text: From the time I brought your ancestors up from Egypt until today, I warned them again and again, saying, “Obey me.”
For I solemnly warned your fathers when I brought them up out of the land of Egypt, warning them persistently, even to this day, saying, Obey my voice.
“Listen to the terms of this covenant and tell them to the people of Judah and to those who live in Jerusalem.
“Hear the words of this covenant, and speak to the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
